chapters.title: The Breast-Meat
chapters.text:
Long ago there lived a rich man named Ernazar, master of great flocks and herds, and father of eight strong sons. But a cruel winter of famine — a zhut — came upon the land. The men drove the beasts to far pastures, and there they vanished; month upon month passed, and no word came. The old couple's food ran out, and hunger bent them low.
One evening the old woman opened the smoke-hole of the yurt, and Ernazar, too weak to rise, looked up at the shanyrak — and there, forgotten on the roof-poles, hung a dried strip of mare's breast-meat, fat and good.
Ernazar: “Wife — glad tidings! The breast-meat on the shanyrak looks fat and fine. Quick, boil it, quick!”
Mother: “Bless the day. We shall eat, old man, and live to see the spring.”
They boiled the meat and ate, and strength returned to them. Not long after, the old woman found herself with child, and when her time came she bore a son. Because a strip of breast-meat — töstik — had given him life, they named him Töstik.
And Töstik was no common child. In one month he grew as a one-year-old, in three months as a boy of three, and in a single year he stood tall as a lad of fifteen. By two years old he could throw a grown man, and send an arrow clean through nine layers of iron. He hunted beast and bird, and fed his old father and mother well.

chapters.title: The Weaver's Taunt
chapters.text:
One day, chasing a wounded bird, Töstik leapt over an old woman's weaving-loom and his heel snapped its threads. The old woman flew into a temper.
Mother: “May you wither in your prime! You tear an old woman's work, yet you cannot find the eight brothers who strayed off and were lost. Go find them, if you are a man!”
Er-Töstik: “Eight brothers? I have no brothers. What are you saying, old mother?”
The words struck him to the heart. He ran home and pressed his mother, and at last she told him the truth: eight elder brothers, driven off with the herds in the year of the zhut, never returned. From that hour Töstik knew no rest.
Er-Töstik: “Then I will find them, Mother — every one. Dry me meat for the road; my bow will feed me the rest of the way.”

chapters.title: The Eight Brothers
chapters.text:
Töstik walked for months and years, across many lands and peoples, his bow both his companion and his larder. When his iron boots had worn to coins and his iron staff to a needle, he came at last upon a great dark herd of horses, and beyond it a people holding a memorial feast.
Ragged and thin, no one heeded the wanderer. But among the dishes he heard servers cry, 'A portion for Ernazar's eight! A portion for Ernazar's eight!' His heart leapt. He gave his name and his father's, and the eight brothers, weeping, knew their youngest at last.
The nine set out to drive the huge herd home, but the horses would not move as one; however they were driven, they scattered and circled back. For days the brothers laboured in vain, until Töstik found a cunning way.
Er-Töstik: “Catch me the lead mare — the dun. If she walks, the whole herd will follow her of its own accord.”
He bound the dun mare and twisted a hair-rope about her thigh; she screamed, and at her cry the whole herd gathered close. Then Töstik led her by the halter, and the great herd streamed after in a living river. Drinking the clearest water, grazing the sweetest grass, the nine brothers came safely home, and Ernazar held a feast that filled the steppe.

chapters.title: Nine Brides and the Peri
chapters.text:
Ernazar set out to find nine brides for his nine sons — nine sisters of one mother, he wished. In a great brown yurt he saw eight earrings hung on the lattice and wept for the ninth that was missing; but the mistress fetched a ninth, the earring of her youngest, Kenzhekei, whom she kept ever a little apart. So Kenzhekei was matched to Töstik.
Thirty days of games, forty days of feasting, and the nine brides were led home with rich dowries. But on the road a peri-maiden named Bektory saw Töstik, and burned to have him for herself, and set her heart on tearing him from Kenzhekei.
Bektory the Peri: “A hero like you, wasted on a mortal bride? The world under and over the earth could be yours, Töstik — with me.”
Er-Töstik: “I know my own heart, peri. It is promised, and a batyr does not break his word.”

chapters.title: The Ogress at the Well
chapters.text:
Wise Kenzhekei begged the caravan not to camp at the ill-omened well of Sorqudyq. But Ernazar, unwilling to be ruled by a new daughter-in-law, camped there all the same. In the morning the grey she-camel had strayed, and Ernazar rode out alone to find her.
He found the camel by a lone tree, its lead-rope tangled — and beneath the tree crouched a pitiful old woman. 'Mother, hand me up the rope,' he said. As he bent, the crone seized his throat with a grip like iron. It was a jalmauyz, an ogress, and she meant to crush the life from him.
Ernazar: “Spare me, mother! Take my sheep, my camels, my horses, my eight sons — anything! Only let me go.”
The Jalmauyz Ogress: “No. None of that. Give me Töstik — your ninth, your only miracle — and you may live.”
Half-dead, Ernazar promised. The ogress named her lure: Töstik could never travel without his bowman's file; Ernazar would leave it here, and when the boy came to fetch it, she would take him. Kenzhekei, hidden nearby, saw and heard everything.

chapters.title: Shalkuiryk and the File
chapters.text:
Back home, Kenzhekei set a knife-point to Töstik's heart and its handle to her own, and would not let him move until he heard the truth: his father had bargained him away to the ogress, and the lost file was the trap. 'Ask him for the file,' she said, 'and watch him stammer.'
Kenzhekei: “Do not take the ambling ponies your father offers. Ask for Shalkuiryk, the tulpar; ask for the Aksyrmaly armour. Trust the horse, and trust me.”
Er-Töstik: “Then Shalkuiryk it shall be. If the file is bait, I will spring the trap and walk out of it whole.”
No man could catch Shalkuiryk — he bit and kicked all who came near — but to Töstik he bowed his head to the bridle of his own accord. Kenzhekei knelt the grey she-camel and swore an oath: it would not rise until the day Töstik returned, alive or dead. Then horse and hero rode out; and once the aul was behind them, Shalkuiryk spoke.
Shalkuiryk: “From now, batyr, our two souls are one. The ogress waits by your file. When you reach for it, distract her — ask if the girls behind her are hers. I will shrink lower than the wormwood; snatch the file, and never look back.”
It fell out as the horse foretold. 'Mother — are all those girls behind you your own?' Töstik asked; the crone glanced back, and in that instant he snatched the file and Shalkuiryk sprang away. The ogress gave chase, mountains flying like tumbleweed, hail battering down; a boulder the size of a cauldron wrapped Shalkuiryk's tail, and under the weight of his galloping the earth cracked open — and horse and hero plunged into the world below.

chapters.title: The Snake-Khan
chapters.text:
Under the earth, Shalkuiryk warned his rider: at the court of Bapy Khan, serpents would rise from every side; to flinch was to be shamed forever, and never to see the sun again. Töstik left the horse apart and walked into the serpent-hall alone.
Two black serpents hissed up at the door — he did not blink. Two grey ones slid into his sleeve and out at his collar, in at his breast and out at his boot — he did not blink. Two golden serpents reared from the throne — and still he did not blink, but sat down calmly in the seat of honour.
Bapy, the Snake-Khan: “Well come, Er-Töstik of the upper world! You did not flinch, and so no one under the earth shall wrong you. My champions and craftsmen are at your side.”
Er-Töstik: “Then I am honoured, Bapy Khan. Ask what you will of me; a guest repays his welcome.”
The Snake-Khan had long sought the daughter of Temir Khan and been refused. If Töstik would fetch her, Bapy would give his own daughter and untold treasure. Töstik agreed, and set out across seven months of empty country toward the aul of Temir Khan.

chapters.title: The Six Companions
chapters.text:
On the long road Töstik met, one after another, six marvels of the underworld, each waiting to join the hero of whom all the lower world now spoke. First the Deft One, who could steal the tails from two magpies and swap them unseen; then Swift-Foot, so fast he tied a cauldron-stone to his leg to keep from overshooting the game.
Zhelayaq the Swift: “They say the upper-world Töstik has fallen below. I have waited on this road to be his man.”
Er-Töstik: “Then wait no longer, Swift-Foot — the Töstik you seek is here. Walk with me.”
So too came Mountain-Mover, who lifted whole peaks for sport; Lake-Drinker, who swallowed rivers and let them out again; Keen-Ear, who heard every secret whispered in the world; and Sharp-Eye, who could see through mountains. Seven now, they crossed the last of the waste and came to the land of Temir Khan.

chapters.title: The Contests of Temir Khan
chapters.text:
Temir Khan would give his daughter only to the one who bested his whole realm. So the trials began. At the feast meant to shame the newcomers, Lake-Drinker alone ate everything set before seven of them; when poison was slipped into their dishes, Keen-Ear heard the plot, and the Deft One quietly moved the poisoned bowls to the khan's own men, who died of it.
Temir Khan: “Very well. Beaten at the table — then let us race horses. My swiftest against yours, over a fortnight of ground.”
Er-Töstik: “Shalkuiryk will run it. Only string a triple rope across the finish, khan — you will see why.”
Shalkuiryk ran three days and nights and came in like a whirlwind, breaking two ropes of the three; he fell as though dead, but at Töstik's cry — 'You had three lives, one is surely left — rise, Shalkuiryk!' — the tulpar breathed and stood. Then Mountain-Mover threw every wrestler and hoisted a peak overhead; and in the foot-race Swift-Foot, tricked into sleep by a sly hag, woke, flung dust in her eyes, and flew past her to win.
At the last, Temir Khan bid them raise his giant cauldron, sunk to the bottom of a deep lake. Lake-Drinker could not swallow it dry, so Shalkuiryk dived. White foam — good; red foam — ill. First white rose, then red; Töstik wept — until the tulpar surfaced, the great black cauldron wrapped in his lengthened tail.

chapters.title: The Iron Trap
chapters.text:
Beaten at every turn, Temir Khan and his ally feigned surrender and led the guests into a great hall built wholly of iron. The doors clanged shut; mountains of firewood were heaped outside and set alight, and the iron walls began to glow.
Tausogar the Mountain-Mover: “Iron door? I have opened mountains. Stand back!”
Koltauysar the Lake-Drinker: “And I still hold half a lake in one cheek — let the fire drink it!”
Mountain-Mover kicked the iron door flat; Lake-Drinker spat out a whole lake and drowned every flame. Their treachery undone, Temir Khan yielded his daughter at last and sped the wedding-party back toward Bapy Khan. There the Snake-Khan, overjoyed, gave Töstik his own daughter, and asked what more the hero desired.
Er-Töstik: “Only one thing, great khan — the sun. Show me the road to the upper world, and let me go home.”

chapters.title: The Great Bird Samuryk
chapters.text:
The road to the upper world was six months long, and so cruel that Bapy's daughter and all the escort perished; only Töstik, Shalkuiryk, and a serving-girl named Kunke came through. They rested at a spring beneath a colossal world-tree, the Bayterek — and from its crown came the shrieking of terrified chicks.
A dragon was winding up the trunk to devour them, as it did each year. Töstik loosed one arrow between its eyes, and it fell dead. The chicks grew calm — and then a storm rose, and rain, and a wind like a gale: the great two-headed bird Samuryk, one wing a month's ride wide, come home to her nest.
Samuryk, the Great Bird: “A man, at the root of my tree? Did you come to harm my young? — No. My chicks tell it: you slew the dragon that has robbed my nest a hundred years. Ask what you will, friend.”
Er-Töstik: “Only to see the sun again, great bird. Carry me to the world above.”
Samuryk bore Töstik on her wing through the narrow gap into daylight, and at parting gave him one feather of power. 'Burn this in a hopeless hour, and I will come from across the world to save you. Lose it, and look for me no more.'

chapters.title: The Hidden Soul
chapters.text:
But in the upper world a new danger waited. The peri Bektory, cheated of Töstik, had strangled the old ogress and set the ogress's son — Shoyynkulak, Iron-Ear — to guard the place where Töstik would surface. He seized the sleeping hero, bound him, and cast him into a pit; Shalkuiryk he chained, and Kunke he took for his own.
For years Töstik lay in the pit — until Shalkuiryk, tethered with a leather belt, broke loose and stamped above him; a fire-striker fell down to him, and Töstik lit Samuryk's feather. The great bird came, lifted him free, and told him a secret: Shoyynkulak could not be killed, for he kept his soul hidden far outside his body. Kunke drew the secret from the monster through his own child, and told Töstik.
The secret ran thus: forty deer come at noon to Boryqty spring; among them a bent brown doe; in her belly nine chests; in the smallest chest nine small birds — and those nine birds are the monster's soul. Töstik lay in wait, shot the brown doe as she drank, seized the smallest chest, and it held nine fluttering birds.
He tore the necks of eight, and Shoyynkulak fell gasping to the ground. As the monster's strength failed, Töstik faced him one last time. Now — spell the word that names what Töstik has been from birth to the final blow.
With the last bird's neck wrung, Iron-Ear fell dead, and his wicked child with him. Töstik was free at last of the peri and her monsters. He mounted Shalkuiryk and turned for home — and far away, the grey she-camel began, at long last, to calve.

chapters.title: The Batyr Comes Home
chapters.text: So Er-Töstik broke free of the peri's ogress and her iron-eared son, and turned at last for home. Many years had passed under the earth; only Kenzhekei still watched the horizon, sure he would come. When the grey she-camel calved and a lame grey horse whinnied on the ridge, she knew Shalkuiryk's voice — and knew her batyr had returned. In that hour the years fell away: Töstik a young man again, Kenzhekei a new bride, Shalkuiryk a five-year steed. And they lived long, and reached all they had ever wished.
